title: Loss Spikes and Divergence
concept: During months of pretraining, the loss can suddenly spike to infinity, ruining weeks of training if not caught and corrected.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a major risk that can ruin months of AI pretraining engineers face during months of training teams face during months of training runs face-palming risk when training a large AI model for months?
exercises.explain: During long training runs, loss spikes can occur suddenly and ruin all progress, making them a major challenge for AI developers.

cards.title: The Training Nightmare
cards.body: Imagine spending millions of dollars and months of computer time training a super-smart AI, only for a sudden mathematical glitch to wipe out all its brainpower in a fraction of a second. This is the nightmare of loss spikes!

cards.title: What is a Loss Spike?
cards.body: A loss spike happens when the model's error suddenly jumps to an extremely high number (or infinity) during training. This is called 'divergence.' It means the AI's internal settings have broken down, making its guesses completely useless.

cards.title: The Mountain Slip
cards.body: Imagine you are slowly climbing a steep, narrow mountain path. Suddenly, you trip on a loose rock and slide all the way down to the bottom. In AI training, a loss spike is that sudden, catastrophic trip that completely ruins all the climbing progress you made over weeks of hard work.

cards.title: The Day the Model Broke
cards.body: Imagine a large language model that has been training smoothly for two months. Suddenly, on day 62, a batch of unusual data causes a massive math error. The loss spikes to infinity, and from that moment on, the model only outputs gibberish like 'NaN NaN NaN'. weeks of training are wasted.

cards.title: Myth: Spikes Heal Themselves
cards.body: It is easy to think that if we just let the AI keep training after a loss spike, it will eventually recover. In reality, once a model 'diverges' and hits infinity, its internal math is permanently broken. You must stop, load a saved backup, and change settings to fix it.

cards.title: Stable vs. Diverged Training
cards.body: Visualizing the difference between stable training and a catastrophic loss spike.

left.text: Loss steadily goes down over weeks. The model gets smarter.

right.text: Loss suddenly shoots straight up to infinity. The model breaks.

exercises.prompt: What is a loss spike in the context of AI pretraining?

options.text: A sudden, extreme jump in the model's error rate to infinity
A rapid improvement in how fast the AI learns
A planned pause to let the training computers cool down
A small wiggle in the graph that fixes itself immediately

exercises.explain: A loss spike is a sudden, extreme jump in error that signals the model's training has broken down (diverged).

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model experiences divergence and its loss spikes, it will always fix itself if you just keep training.
exercises.explain: Once a model's loss spikes to infinity, the internal math is broken and it cannot recover without loading a previous healthy save state (checkpoint).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the definition of training divergence.
exercises.sentence: During divergence, the training loss spikes all the way to [blank], ruining the model.
exercises.explain: When training diverges, the loss math breaks down entirely, pushing the error value up to infinity (often seen as NaN, or Not-a-Number, in code).

exercises.prompt: Identify which statement contains false AI-generated advice about loss spikes.

options.text: Human Statement: 'A loss spike can ruin weeks of training if you do not have a checkpoint saved.'
AI Statement: 'Loss spikes are normal, and the AI will naturally recover its intelligence if you leave it running.'
Human Statement: 'Gradient clipping is one way we try to prevent these spikes from breaking the model.'

exercises.explain: The AI-generated statement is incorrect because once a model diverges and loss hits infinity, it cannot recover on its own; it requires human intervention and restoring from a checkpoint.

exercises.prompt: During a massive pretraining run, you notice the loss is starting to climb wildly toward infinity. Which safety mechanism is commonly used to prevent these mathematical updates from destroying your model?

options.text: Speeding up training so it finishes before a spike happens
Gradient clipping to cap the maximum size of updates
Deleting the dataset and starting over from scratch
Using smaller computer screens to hide the visual spikes

exercises.explain: Gradient clipping caps the maximum size of updates during training. This acts as a safety barrier so that when the AI encounters a massive mathematical bump, it won't make a huge update that ruins the entire model.
